title: Pre-felt collage sampler
subtitle: Layer and fuse cut pre-felt shapes onto a wet-felted background to build colour pictures
excerpt: Pre-felt is partially felted wool that holds its shape when cut but hasn't fully shrunk. Cut it into precise shapes, arrange them on a wet-felted background, then complete the felting pass to lock every piece flat and bond the edges cleanly into the base. This sampler panel is approximately 25 x 20 cm finished.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; the pre-felt collage technique is demonstrated across guild workshops and in standard wet-felting teaching curricula.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool that has been partially wet-felted so the fibres have begun to mat and the sheet holds its shape when cut. Pre-felt is firm enough to cut into precise shapes with scissors but still open enough to bond with neighbouring wool during a subsequent felting pass. Commercial pre-felt is sold by the sheet; you can make your own by stopping a standard wet-felting session about halfway through.

Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope or strip, ready for spinning or felting. In wet felting, wisps are pulled from roving and laid in overlapping layers. Merino roving felts readily and produces a smooth, even surface.

content.text: A pre-felt collage sampler uses two separate stages of felting to build pictures with clean-edged shapes. In the first stage you make or buy
pre-felt
sheets in several colours. In the second stage you cut shapes from those sheets, arrange them on a freshly laid
roving

background, and complete the felting to lock everything flat. Because the pre-felt pieces are already partially compressed, they hold their cut edges during the final felting pass rather than spreading and blurring the way raw roving would. The finished panel is approximately 25 x 20 cm.
What you need

attrs.name: Merino roving, background colour (such as cream or grey), 60 g
Pre-felt sheets in 3 to 4 contrasting colours, each at least 15 x 15 cm
Felting mat, at least 30 x 25 cm
Felting net or net curtain piece, approx. 35 x 30 cm

content.text: Making or sourcing pre-felt

Commercial pre-felt is sold by craft suppliers in A4 or A3 sheets in a range of colours. It is the straightforward option for a first collage sampler. If you want to make your own, lay a thin two-layer square of coloured merino roving, wet it out with soapy water, cover it with net, and rub it gently for 3 to 4 minutes until the surface felts but the sheet still bends easily and feels soft throughout. Stop before it stiffens completely. Rinse and dry the sheet flat. Homemade pre-felt bonds slightly better with the base layer because the fibres are at an equivalent state of matting, but commercial pre-felt bonds reliably when the two surfaces are both thoroughly saturated during the collage pass.
Designing your composition

Keep the first sampler simple: two or three large geometric shapes or simple silhouettes (leaves, circles, squares). Cut the shapes slightly larger than you want in the finished panel, as they will shrink a further 15 to 20 percent during the final felting pass. Thin cut lines (lines narrower than 5 mm) may seal over during felting and disappear, so keep any cut-out details bold. Sketch the composition on paper and label which colour goes where before cutting into the pre-felt sheets.
Method
Step 1: Lay the background panel

Lay the felting mat on a stable surface. Pull thin wisps from the background roving and lay them horizontally across a 35 x 28 cm area in two layers, then add two more layers at 90 degrees to the first pair. You need four thin overlapping layers in total. Keep the layer thickness even. Extend the wisps about 5 cm beyond the boundary at each edge; these overhanging fibres will be folded back in Step 3 to neaten the panel edge. Do not wet the panel yet.
Step 2: Cut and place the pre-felt shapes

Cut the pre-felt sheets into the planned shapes. Cut with one smooth scissor movement rather than short snips; jagged cuts produce fuzzier edges after felting. Lay the cut shapes onto the dry background roving in the planned positions. Press each piece down gently with a flat palm. If a shape needs repositioning, lift it cleanly before wetting: wet pre-felt tears easily. Once you are satisfied with the arrangement, fold the overhanging edge wisps back over the outermost pre-felt shapes to secure the panel border.
Step 3: Cover with net and wet out

Lay the felting net over the entire panel. Mix the olive-oil soap into the hot water and pour slowly from a height of about 30 cm over the net-covered panel, distributing the water evenly across the whole surface. Press down steadily with flat palms to saturate without displacing shapes. The pre-felt pieces should turn visibly darker when fully saturated. Check the edges: they need as much water as the centre. Tip the mat slightly to drain pooling water.
Step 4: Rub and felt

Rub through the net in gentle flat-palm circles, starting at the centre and working outward. In the first two minutes the pre-felt shapes may try to shift slightly; keep your palm over a shape if it moves, pressing it back flat before continuing. After about 3 minutes of rubbing, lift the net carefully from one corner and check that the shapes have bonded to the background. A bonded shape resists being lifted by a fingernail; an unbonded shape peels away cleanly. Replace the net and continue rubbing if any shapes are still loose. Once all shapes are bonded, remove the net and rub the surface directly with a soapy palm for a further 3 minutes.
Step 5: Full and shrink to size

Roll the panel up in the felting mat and roll the mat back and forth on the table with firm palm pressure for 3 minutes. Unroll, rotate the panel 90 degrees, and roll again. Measure after every two rolling passes. The panel is done when it has shrunk to approximately 25 x 20 cm and the surface feels firm and uniformly dense. Shock with cold water twice during the rolling stage to accelerate shrinkage. The pre-felt shapes will be clearly visible with sharper edges than raw-roving inlay would produce.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

Rinse in lukewarm water until the water runs clear and no soap remains. Press excess water out by rolling the panel in a clean tea towel and pressing firmly; do not wring. Lay flat on a dry tea towel, pull gently to square, and dry at room temperature. The finished panel can be framed under glass, made into a card front, or used as the face of a small pouch.
Common problems

attrs.problem: The pre-felt shapes have fuzzy or blurred edges in the finished panel
attrs.cause: The pre-felt was positioned on top of a thick outer roving layer, or the rubbing motion was circular and moved fibres laterally into the shape boundaries
attrs.fix: Use the thinnest possible top roving layer (one layer only) over any area where a crisp edge is needed. Rub through the net rather than directly on the surface in the early stage to reduce lateral fibre movement.

attrs.problem: A pre-felt shape lifts away during the rubbing stage and folds or creases
attrs.cause: The shape was not fully saturated before rubbing began, so the surface fibres didn't interlock with the background
attrs.fix: Re-wet the lifted area thoroughly, press the shape flat with your palm, and continue rubbing. If the shape has torn, smooth it back into position while damp.

attrs.problem: The panel has shrunk unevenly, with one axis much shorter than the other
attrs.cause: Rolling was done in one direction only across the entire fulling stage
attrs.fix: Rotate 90 degrees between every rolling pass without exception. If the panel is already lopsided, continue rolling in the longer axis only until both dimensions match.
